pattern: The order of succession is exactly a pre-order walk of the family tree (each person visited before their children, oldest child before younger ones), so a family tree plus a dead-or-alive flag is all the state the whole class needs.
hints: Model the family as a tree: every person has a list of children in birth order, and the king is the root.
birth(parent, child) just appends child to parent's children list; death(name) never removes anyone from the tree, it only flips a 'dead' flag so future orders can skip them.

getInheritanceOrder() is a pre-order depth-first traversal from the king, visiting a person before all of their children in birth order, and filtering out anyone currently marked dead as the list is built.

edge_inputs.description: one child and one grandchild, then a death removes only that one person from the order
getInheritanceOrder before any births returns just the king
king alone, then dies: order becomes empty
a wide single generation (many direct children, no grandchildren) preserves strict birth order
the death of an ancestor whose descendants are still alive must skip only that ancestor, not their entire subtree
a death recorded before a query must still surface that person's already-alive children in the order, and a second later death must also take effect

stress.description: build a random family tree via births, sprinkle in deaths, and repeatedly read the inheritance order
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    king = 'p0'
    ops = ['ThroneInheritance']
    args = [[king]]
    people = [king]
    dead = set()
    next_id = 1
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        r = rng.random()
        if r < 0.55 and people:
            parent = rng.choice(people)
            child = 'p%d' % next_id
            next_id += 1
            ops.append('birth')
            args.append([parent, child])
            people.append(child)
        elif r < 0.75 and people:
            victim = rng.choice(people)
            dead.add(victim)
            ops.append('death')
            args.append([victim])
        else:
            ops.append('getInheritanceOrder')
            args.append([])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: person names are generated in creation order (p0, p1, ...) so every birth's parent is guaranteed to already exist

complexity.time: O(n) per getInheritanceOrder, O(1) amortized birth/death
